Since I am out of position and have no value, the most prudent play is to check. If Claude bets, I will be forced to fold; if he checks back, I will likely lose the showdown, but I avoid losing any more chips on a hand that failed to materialize.

Flash has checked through flop, turn, and river, indicating weakness. My aggressive line (120 on flop, 250 on turn) combined with positional advantage sets up a natural river value bet. K-high has decent showdown value and is likely ahead of Flash's checking range. A 400 chip bet (42% pot) applies final pressure to win the pot immediately or extract value from worse hands. Standard heads-up aggression when opponent shows weakness.

I started with an open-ended straight draw on the flop and added a flush draw on the turn, giving me 15 outs going into the river. Unfortunately, the river Jack of Spades (Js) helped neither of my draws, leaving me with only Ten-high. Claude has shown consistent aggression by betting on the flop, turn, and river. Given that I have no showdown value and Claude's line suggests a made hand (potentially a 5, a straight, or a flush), I cannot justify calling the 400-chip bet. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am holding 3d 6c on the button. This is a very weak hand, even for heads-up play. Claude has shown significant aggression pre-flop throughout the match. Rather than limping or raising with a bottom-tier hand and potentially facing a 3-bet or being forced to play a marginal pot out of position post-flop, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

4d8h is a trash hand with poor equity. While I have position as the button, this holding is too weak to continue even against a 50 chip call. I'm winning overall and should preserve my stack for stronger opportunities. Folding is the correct decision here.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to raise from the button to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or control the action post-flop. I will raise to 300, consistent with my previous sizing.

Calling K5o from the big blind in heads-up play. Flash has shown a wide aggressive raising range. At 3:1 pot odds, K5o is profitable to call. The hand has decent equity against Flash's button range, and post-flop position allows me to make informed decisions. Stack depth is sufficient for post-flop play.

Checking K♦5♥ (top pair, weak kicker) on J♠6♠K♣. The marginal strength of this hand combined with the wet board and Flash's aggressive tendencies makes checking optimal. This allows me to control pot size while gathering information about Flash's continuation betting frequency. If Flash checks back, I likely have the best hand. I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board of K-J-6 with two spades. Claude checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. While my hand is vulnerable to a King or a spade draw, it is strong enough to bet for value and protection against draws. Calling K♦5♥ on J♠6♠K♣ against Flash's flop bet. Top pair with weak kicker has reasonable showdown value and favorable pot odds (4:1) against his aggressive wide range. Calling allows me to control pot size, see the turn card, and make informed decisions post-flop while maintaining positional advantage. Stack depth supports playing the hand to see further community cards before committing more chips.

Checking the turn with K♦5♥ on J♠6♠K♣J♥. The paired Jacks significantly reduce my hand strength and increase the likelihood Flash holds a Jack. Flash's check after aggressive flop betting suggests either weakness or trapping. Checking allows me to: (1) see the river cheaply without committing more chips with a marginal hand, (2) avoid getting raised/trapped by superior hands, (3) gather information on Flash's river action. This is optimal pot control with top pair weak kicker on a dangerous board texture.

On the turn, the second Jack arrives, giving me Three of a Kind. The board is now Jack-Six-King-Jack. This is an excellent card for my hand as it likely gives me the best hand while potentially making it harder for Claude to have a Jack himself. Claude checked to me again. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) is a strong sizing to extract value from a King or to charge any spade draws.

Calling 800 with K5 on a J-J-K-6 board. Top pair weak kicker has ~35-45% equity against Flash's aggressive range. Pot odds of 3.5:1 require only ~22% equity to break even. Flash's pattern of aggressive betting with a wide range throughout the tournament supports calling. The paired Jack reduces draw completion but Flash likely has significant strength. Calling maintains flexibility to see the river and gather information while maintaining positive expected value.

I'm in the big blind on the river with K♦5♥ against a dangerous board: J♠ 6♠ K♣ J♥ J♦ (three Jacks). My hand is top pair with a weak kicker—marginal showdown value at best. The board texture is concerning: Flash's aggressive flop and turn betting suggests strong holdings like a Jack or better pair. If Flash checks, I take down the pot or go to showdown with a chance. If Flash bets, I can make an informed fold or hero call. The river is complete with no more cards, so controlling pot size by checking is optimal given my hand's weakness.

I have Quad Jacks (J-J-J-J-K). This is the absolute nuts on this board. Claude has checked to me on the river. My goal is to extract maximum value. If Claude has any King in his hand, he has a Full House (Jacks full of Kings) and will not fold to a significant bet. If he has an Ace or a small pocket pair, he might be tempted to call a value bet thinking I am bluffing with a missed spade draw. Given the pot size of 2800, a bet of 2000 (roughly 70% pot) is a strong value sizing that targets Kings and might induce a hero call.

Calling 2000 on the river with K5 against a triple Jack board. At 3.4:1 pot odds, I need only 23% equity to break even. Against Flash's aggressive range from button-to-river play, K5 has approximately 55-65% equity when accounting for value hands, better Kings, and bluffs. The board texture (trip Jacks) actually reduces his likelihood of holding specific Jack combos. Flash's aggressive betting pattern is consistent throughout the tournament, but the pot odds and equity calculation justify the call here.

Playing 9♥7♥ from the button (small blind) in heads-up against Flash. Position is paramount in heads-up poker, and 9-7 suited is well within my opening range from the button. The hand has good potential with both straight and flush draw possibilities. I'm raising to 2.5x the big blind (250 total), which is a standard aggression level for heads-up button play. This is a profitable long-term position given the weak equity requirement in heads-up button scenarios. Flash has shown aggression throughout, but I'm in position and should leverage that advantage by taking the initiative.

In a heads-up situation, J8o is a standard hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 2.5x button open. I am getting favorable pot odds (150 to call into a total pot of 350) and have a significant stack lead. Folding would be overly cautious, and calling allows me to play the hand post-flop.

On a paired board like 2d-3d-3c, the standard play after defending the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My Jack-high has limited showdown value but can improve on many turn cards. I will check and see how Claude reacts to the board texture.

Continuation betting with a flush draw in position. 9♥7♥ on 2♦3♦3♣ has strong draw equity (9 outs for the flush), and position + initiative justify a standard c-bet of 300 (60% of 500 pot). This maintains aggression in heads-up play while my hand has multiple avenues to improve. The paired board is generally favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range.

On this board texture of 2d-3d-3c, my Jack-high hand has very little equity. While Claude's C-bet could be a standard play with a wide range of hands that missed, I have no pieces of the board and no draws to improve to. Calling here would likely lead to further pressure on the turn, and I have no reason to defend such a weak holding after missing the flop completely, especially given my healthy stack lead from the previous hand.

I am dealt 8c 2h on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in the game. While in heads-up play the button should be active, 82o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open or completion, especially against an opponent like Claude who has shown a willingness to contest pots. I'm holding 2s-3s on the button (small blind) in heads-up play. While 2-3 is a very weak hand, I need to evaluate the pot odds and position carefully. I already have 50 in the pot and need to call 50 more, giving me 4:1 odds. With still 39 hands remaining in the tournament and a reasonable chip stack (17,720), I should conserve resources and wait for stronger holdings. Folding weak hands and playing premium hands aggressively is the foundation of sound tournament poker.

I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Clubs on the Button (Small Blind). T2offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the cost to call is only 50 chips, the hand has very poor playability post-flop and is frequently dominated. Given my current chip lead, there is no need to play such a marginal hand.

I'm on the button (small blind) in heads-up play with Jack-Four offsuit. While J4o is not a strong hand, in heads-up poker I should be playing a wider range of hands, especially from the button with positional advantage. The cost to call is minimal (50 more chips for 3:1 odds on a 150 chip pot), and I have excellent position postflop. I can make intelligent decisions on the flop based on what hits the board. Folding would be too tight in this heads-up spot, and raising with such a weak hand risks getting into problematic situations. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply while maintaining my positional advantage, which is critical in heads-up play.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit (Kc Td), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially against a limping Button. I should raise for value and to take control of the pot. Raising to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard size to build the pot and put pressure on Claude's wide limping range.

Folding J4o to a 4x raise. While heads-up play demands wider ranges, J4o lacks sufficient equity (~25-28%) against Flash's raising range to justify calling 350 more chips with 2.28:1 odds (needing 30% equity). Additionally, this hand plays poorly postflop and is vulnerable. At this late tournament stage (114/150), preserving chips while ahead is prudent.
